title: Feature-Market Fit — Other Commercial and Service Industry Machinery Manufacturing (NAICS 333318)

sections.title: Step 09 — Technology-Level Feature-Market Fit
sections.content:
7 technology-scope features assessed against NAICS 333318. Kano classification + 9-dimension ODI fit scoring grounded in the 33 error statements from JTBD_other_commercial_and_service_industry_ma.

**Commodity FP baseline:** "Quantify the volumetric or mass flow rate of fluids traversing a closed conduit" — features delivering this are the must_be baseline.

**Market segments:** High-volume vended laundry, on-premises institutional laundry, express tunnel car wash, in-bay/self-service car wash, commercial water purification, commercial ice & beverage dispensing. [SRC: JTBD_other_commercial_and_service_industry_ma §1.2]

**Incumbent landscape:** Turbine/paddle-wheel 45–55% share, solenoid-valve timed fill 30–40%, electromagnetic 3–5%, ultrasonic <5%. [SRC: JTBD_other_commercial_and_service_industry_ma §2.4]

### 09a — Kano + ODI Scores

| Feature | Kano | Time | Cost | Safety | Reli | Skill | Stress | Pain | Conf | Delight | Fit |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Transit-Time Flow Measurement** | must_be | 4 | 6 | 5 | 7 | 3 | 4 | 3 | 7 | 2 | **4.56** |
| **Integrated Temperature Sensing** | attractive | 3 | 5 | 4 | 5 | 4 | 3 | 4 | 5 | 4 | **4.11** |
| **Zero Pressure Drop Flow Path** | one_dimensional | 2 | 5 | 2 | 4 | 2 | 2 | 2 | 3 | 2 | **2.67** |
| **No Moving Parts** | attractive | 5 | 8 | 4 | 9 | 6 | 7 | 8 | 7 | 6 | **6.67** |
| **Bidirectional Flow Detection** | attractive | 3 | 5 | 6 | 4 | 2 | 4 | 2 | 5 | 3 | **3.78** |
| **Digital Anomaly Detection** | attractive | 5 | 6 | 8 | 6 | 5 | 7 | 5 | 7 | 5 | **6.00** |
| **Real-Time Compensation Algorithms** | one_dimensional | 2 | 4 | 3 | 7 | 4 | 4 | 3 | 7 | 2 | **4.00** |

### 09a — Rationales

**Transit-Time Flow Measurement** (must_be, 4.56): Directly delivers the commodity FP — measuring water flow is the reason commercial equipment OEMs buy a flow sensor. Without it, no billing, no water-use reporting, no fault detection. Primary drivers: Reliability (7) — continuous quantitative measurement replaces timed-fill guesswork (±10–20% vs ±1–3%); Confidence (7) — provides measured data for pay-per-use billing and WaterSense certification. Grounded in N01 (±3% accuracy, Opp=14) and N08 (per-cycle billing, Opp=13). Must_be because every flowmeter technology provides this. [SRC: JTBD §3.2 N01/N08, 01_product_decomposition]

**Integrated Temperature Sensing** (attractive, 4.11): Commercial washers cycle between 5°C cold fill and 85°C hot wash. Having flow + temperature in one sensor enables thermal energy tracking and temperature-compensated flow accuracy without a separate NTC probe. Primary drivers: Cost (5) — eliminates separate temperature sensor BOM line; Reliability (5) — enables real-time temperature compensation reducing error from ~1.5% to ~0.2%; Confidence (5) — temperature-compensated readings are more trustworthy across the 5–85°C operating envelope. Attractive because incumbents (turbine, timed fill) provide no temperature measurement. Not must_be because the market currently manages without it. Grounded in N09 (measurement failure at 5–85°C, Opp=9). [SRC: JTBD §3.2 N09, 01_product_decomposition]

**Zero Pressure Drop Flow Path** (one_dimensional, 2.67): Commercial equipment has pump-constrained fluid circuits. Sensor flow restriction reduces available pump capacity for the washing/rinsing process. Less restriction = proportionally more capacity available (linear). Primary driver: Cost (5) — eliminates head-loss penalty across multiple measurement points in multi-machine installations. Low scores elsewhere because benefit is purely hydraulic. One_dimensional because more flow restriction is proportionally worse (linear relationship between pressure drop and pump energy). [SRC: 01_product_decomposition]

**No Moving Parts** (attractive, 6.67): The #1 pain point in this market. Turbine/paddle-wheel sensors (45–55% incumbent share) clog from lint, detergent residue, and mineral scale within 12 months. A solid-state sensor with no maintenance schedule is genuinely delightful — most OEM engineers accept mechanical sensor replacement as normal. Primary drivers: Reliability (9) — directly eliminates the dominant failure mode in detergent-laden water (N10 rotor clogging, Opp=14); Cost (8) — eliminates $150–300 quarterly field service cleaning calls per machine (N11, Opp=11); Pain (8) — removes quarterly sensor disassembly that service technicians universally dislike. Attractive because the market currently accepts mechanical wear as an unavoidable trade-off. Not must_be — absence doesn't prevent basic function; turbine sensors work, they just fail faster. [SRC: JTBD §3.2 N10/N11/N12, 01_product_decomposition]

**Bidirectional Flow Detection** (attractive, 3.78): Relevant in this market for detecting check-valve failures (causing backflow and water waste), monitoring water recycling system flow direction, and preventing potable water contamination in purification equipment. Primary driver: Safety (6) — detects backflow that could contaminate potable water supply in NSF-regulated purification equipment; Cost (5) — identifies water waste from failed check valves in multi-machine plumbing. Attractive because commercial machinery OEMs don't expect directional flow sensing — check valves are used instead. Not indifferent (unlike breweries) because 333318 has multi-machine plumbing with check valves that fail silently. Grounded in N06 (undetected water leaks, Opp=13). [SRC: JTBD §3.2 N06]

**Digital Anomaly Detection** (attractive, 6.00): Detects air bubbles, empty pipes, and medium composition changes — capabilities that address the top safety pain points. Empty-pipe detection within 5 seconds prevents dry-run damage to pumps and heating elements in unmanned vended laundry and car wash equipment. Bubble detection flags recycled water aeration issues. Primary drivers: Safety (8) — prevents pump/heater destruction from dry-run (N04, Opp=14); Stress (7) — dramatically reduces anxiety about undetected failures in unmanned equipment; Confidence (7) — definitive diagnostic flags replace manual inspection rounds. Attractive because incumbents (turbine, timed fill) have zero diagnostic capability. N15b (undetected faults with timed fill, Opp=14) is entirely eliminated by any sensor with anomaly detection. [SRC: JTBD §3.2 N04/N15b, 01_product_decomposition]

**Real-Time Compensation Algorithms** (one_dimensional, 4.00): Compensates for temperature variation (5–85°C), detergent concentration, and recycled water property changes. Better compensation = proportionally better accuracy across the operating envelope. Primary drivers: Reliability (7) — maintains accuracy across rapid thermal cycling in commercial washers (N09, Opp=9); Confidence (7) — users trust compensated readings across variable water quality (N22 recycled water, Opp=13). One_dimensional because 0.2% compensated error is proportionally better than 1.5% uncompensated — the benefit scales linearly with compensation quality. [SRC: JTBD §3.2 N09/N22, 01_product_decomposition]

### 09b — Cross-Market Validation

| # | Check | Result | Note |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Kano consistency | **PASS** | Transit-Time = must_be (commodity FP baseline); 4 attractive features validated against 333318 status quo (turbine/timed-fill incumbents lack these capabilities) |
| 2 | Safety-Confidence correlation | **PASS** | Digital Anomaly: safety=8, confidence=7 — consistent (safer systems earn trust) |
| 3 | Skill-Stress correlation | **PASS** | No feature has skill ≥ 7 with stress ≤ 3 |
| 4 | Indifferent score range | **N/A** | No features classified indifferent — all are relevant to this market |
| 5 | No perfect-10 sweep | **PASS** | Highest single score is 9 (No Moving Parts: Reliability) |
| 6 | Feature name compliance | **PASS** | All names are spec-visible noun phrases, 2–6 words, no brands, no superlatives |
| 7 | Overall_fit arithmetic | **PASS** | All recomputed: (sum of 9 scores) / 9, rounded to 2 dp |

**Cross-market note vs Breweries (312120):** Bidirectional Flow Detection = attractive here (3.78) vs indifferent in Breweries (1.89). Justified: 333318 has multi-machine plumbing with check valves that fail silently and potable water contamination risk in purification segments. Breweries have unidirectional glycol loops with no backflow consequence.

---

sections.title: Step 10 — Product-Related Needs (Burleson Product Jobs)
sections.content:
Needs that exist BECAUSE of choosing ultrasonic transit-time technology. Each traces to a specific product constraint from 01_product_decomposition. All pass the litmus test: "Would this need disappear if a different technology were chosen?" = YES.

### 10a — Product-Related Needs

| # | Statement | Source Constraint | Imp | Sat | Litmus Test |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| PR-1 | Minimize the likelihood of ultrasonic signal attenuation when detergent agitation or water recycling aeration generates air entrainment exceeding 2% by volume during commercial wash and rinse cycles | Air Bubble Tolerance | 8 | 4 | YES — electromagnetic flowmeters maintain measurement in aerated conductive fluids |
| PR-2 | Minimize the time to restore valid flow measurement after transient foam or bubble events from detergent injection dissipate from the ultrasonic sensor measurement path | Air Bubble Tolerance | 7 | 3 | YES — Coriolis meters maintain continuous measurement through gas-liquid mixtures |
| PR-3 | Minimize the duration of invalid measurement output during machine drain-to-fill transitions when the ultrasonic sensor encounters partial pipe fill that prevents acoustic signal propagation between transducers | Pipe Full Condition | 7 | 4 | YES — Coriolis sensors degrade gradually rather than total signal loss on partial fill |
| PR-4 | Minimize the measurement error caused by disturbed flow profiles when the ultrasonic sensor is installed with less than 5×DN straight pipe run in compact commercial machine enclosures with elbows and T-junctions upstream | Minimum Straight Pipe Run | 7 | 4 | YES — Coriolis meters are orientation-independent; electromagnetic tolerate 3×DN upstream |
| PR-5 | Minimize the measurement dead zone at trickle-fill and pre-rinse flow rates below 4.2 l/min (DN20) where ultrasonic transit-time difference falls below the signal-to-noise detection threshold | Minimum Flow Rate Detection | 7 | 4 | YES — electromagnetic meters maintain signal at turndown 100:1 vs ultrasonic ~10:1 |
| PR-6 | Minimize the transient measurement error during rapid temperature cycling between cold fill (5°C) and hot wash (85°C) where ultrasonic speed-of-sound correction lags behind actual medium temperature change rate | Temperature-Dependent Accuracy | 7 | 5 | YES — electromagnetic flow measurement is independent of fluid temperature for velocity measurement |
| PR-7 | Minimize the risk of polymer housing seal degradation when commercial hot wash cycles operate at 85°C within 5°C of the ultrasonic sensor's 90°C material limit for extended duty cycles | Maximum Medium Temperature | 6 | 5 | YES — stainless-steel-housed electromagnetic and Coriolis sensors withstand 150°C+ |
| PR-8 | Minimize the likelihood of acoustic coupling degradation when surfactant concentrations in commercial wash water shift the medium's acoustic impedance outside the ultrasonic transducer's validated operating envelope | Media Limitation | 6 | 5 | YES — electromagnetic meters measure velocity via Faraday's law, unaffected by chemical additives |

**Constraints not generating needs for 333318:**

| Constraint | Why Not Applicable |
|---|---|
| Maximum Operating Pressure (10 bar) | Commercial equipment water circuits operate at 2–6 bar (municipal supply). High-pressure car wash spray circuits (50–150 bar) are excluded from sensor scope — different measurement point required [SRC: JTBD §1.5] |

### 10b — Validation

| # | Check | Result | Note |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Litmus test | **PASS** | All 8 needs disappear with a different technology (electromagnetic or Coriolis) |
| 2 | Source constraint traceability | **PASS** | Each constraint exists in 01_product_decomposition; causal mechanism is specific to ultrasonic transit-time operating principle |
| 3 | Affected market plausibility | **PASS** | All constraints manifest in 333318: detergent water (air bubbles, surfactant), compact enclosures (straight run), rapid thermal cycling (5–85°C), drain transitions (pipe full) |
| 4 | Completeness | **PASS** | 7 of 8 constraints generate needs; 1 excluded with rationale (pressure threshold never reached in sensor-applicable circuits) |
| 5 | No market-level duplicates | **PASS** | None duplicate Step 06 P2 market needs from JTBD |
| 6 | Importance/satisfaction sanity | **PASS** | Range 6–8 imp / 3–5 sat; air-bubble needs score highest (imp=8) due to frequent manifestation in detergent/reclaim water |

---

sections.title: Step 11 — Vendor Feature-Market Fit (Marquardt)
sections.content:
6 vendor-scope features compared against best incumbent per feature. Marquardt US Flow Sensor (DN20 serial) vs. best incumbent in 333318 applications.

### 11a — Vendor vs. Incumbent Comparison

| Feature | Kano | Vendor | Incumbent | Inc. Tech | Advantage | Rationale |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Modular Pipe Diameter Variants** | one_dimensional | 6 | 8 | Electromagnetic (E+H Promag) | **inferior** | Marquardt offers 4 variants (DN12/14/20/25) sharing RAST 2.5 electronics; electromagnetic vendors offer DN3–DN300+. DN20/DN25 cover >70% of commercial equipment circuits, but incumbent range is objectively broader. DN12 (ice machines) still in development. Addresses N21 (variant count, Opp=8). |
| **Customizable Communication Interface** | must_be | 7 | 5 | Turbine / Paddle-Wheel | **superior** | Marquardt offers UART/LIN/frequency — matching commercial equipment OEM microcontrollers directly. LIN enables cross-platform OEM development (automotive + commercial). Incumbent turbine sensors provide only passive frequency/pulse output requiring OEM counting/conversion logic. Electromagnetic meters offer 4-20 mA/HART (industrial protocols uncommon in commercial equipment controllers). |
| **Customizable Mechanical Interface** | one_dimensional | 7 | 5 | Turbine / Paddle-Wheel | **superior** | Marquardt's plug-in quick-connect reduces OEM assembly time from 2–3 min (wrench-and-seal threaded) to <30 s (snap-in). For OEMs producing 100+ machines/month, this translates to measurable production cost reduction. Incumbent turbine sensors offer threaded connections only. Addresses N17 (integration time, Opp=9). |
| **Low-Energy Battery Mode** | indifferent | 8 | 2 | Electromagnetic | **superior** | <5 μA standby is well-implemented but commercial water-using machinery is mains-powered. Potential niche for remote standalone car wash water reclaim monitoring. No incumbent addresses this because the need rarely arises in this market. Superior implementation in an irrelevant context. |
| **Pre-Calibrated Delivery** | attractive | 8 | 6 | Electromagnetic (E+H Promag) | **superior** | Marquardt factory-calibrates to customer's specific water/detergent composition and tests across operating flow profile with 100% automated final test. OEMs receive plug-and-produce sensors — no assembly-line calibration step. E+H ships with generic water calibration; field commissioning needed for non-standard media. Addresses N13 (commissioning time, Opp=7) and N17 (integration time, Opp=9). |
| **KTW/WRAS Drinking Water Approval** | one_dimensional | 7 | 6 | Electromagnetic | **parity** | KTW/WRAS enables deployment in potable water circuits within commercial water purification and ice machine segments (segments 5–6). For laundry/car wash (segments 1–4), irrelevant. Electromagnetic meters from major brands carry NSF 61 (US) alongside European certifications. For European OEMs, Marquardt's coverage is fully adequate. \|7-6\| = 1 → parity. |

### 11b — Objectivity Review

| # | Check | Result | Note |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Vendor bias | **PASS** | 4 superior, 1 parity, 1 inferior — 67% superior. Justified: comparison is against turbine incumbent (simple, cheap) for OEM integration features where Marquardt's sophistication provides genuine advantage |
| 2 | Anti-vendor bias | **PASS** | 1 inferior on pipe size range is genuine (electromagnetic covers DN3–DN300+; Marquardt limited to DN12–DN25) |
| 3 | Straw-man check | **PASS** | Best incumbent per feature: E+H Promag (market-leading electromagnetic) for size/calibration/cert; turbine/paddle-wheel (45–55% share incumbent) for communication/mechanical |
| 4 | Advantage arithmetic | **PASS** | All gap calculations verified. Superior requires >1pt vendor-over-incumbent; parity requires |gap| ≤ 1 |
| 5 | Kano-score consistency | **PASS** | Must_be (Comm Interface): both scores ≥ 5 ✓. Attractive (Pre-Calibrated): vendor > incumbent ✓. Indifferent scores reflect capability not market relevance ✓ |
| 6 | Rationale specificity | **PASS** | Each rationale references concrete specs (DN sizes, protocol names, assembly times, calibration methods) |

### Competitive Summary

| Dimension | Marquardt Advantage | Marquardt Disadvantage |
|---|---|---|
| **Pre-Calibrated Delivery** | Eliminates OEM assembly-line calibration — reduces per-unit integration time | — |
| **Communication Interface** | UART/LIN/frequency matches commercial OEM controllers directly | No 4-20 mA or Modbus for industrial-grade PLCs |
| **Mechanical Interface** | Plug-in quick-connect cuts assembly time from minutes to seconds | — |
| **Pipe Size Range** | — | DN12 still in development; no DN50+ for large commercial systems |
| **Battery Mode** | Enables remote monitoring niche | Irrelevant for >95% of mains-powered commercial equipment |

**Key finding:** Marquardt's strongest vendor advantages (Pre-Calibrated Delivery, Communication Interface, Mechanical Interface) directly address the OEM integration pain chain — N13 commissioning time, N17 assembly-line integration, N21 variant count. These advantages compound: a sensor that arrives factory-calibrated, with the right protocol, and snaps into place is fundamentally easier for commercial equipment OEMs to adopt than a turbine sensor requiring wrenching, wiring, and field adjustment. The weakness on pipe size range (inferior vs electromagnetic) is mitigated by the fact that DN20/DN25 covers the majority of commercial equipment circuits.

---

rows.Note: Transit-Time = must_be (commodity FP baseline); 4 attractive features validated against 333318 status quo (turbine/timed-fill incumbents lack these capabilities)
Digital Anomaly: safety=8, confidence=7 — consistent (safer systems earn trust)
No feature has skill ≥ 7 with stress ≤ 3
No features classified indifferent — all are relevant to this market
Highest single score is 9 (No Moving Parts: Reliability)
All names are spec-visible noun phrases, 2–6 words, no brands, no superlatives
All recomputed: (sum of 9 scores) / 9, rounded to 2 dp

rows.Statement: Minimize the likelihood of ultrasonic signal attenuation when detergent agitation or water recycling aeration generates air entrainment exceeding 2% by volume during commercial wash and rinse cycles
rows.Litmus Test: YES — electromagnetic flowmeters maintain measurement in aerated conductive fluids

rows.Statement: Minimize the time to restore valid flow measurement after transient foam or bubble events from detergent injection dissipate from the ultrasonic sensor measurement path
rows.Litmus Test: YES — Coriolis meters maintain continuous measurement through gas-liquid mixtures

rows.Statement: Minimize the duration of invalid measurement output during machine drain-to-fill transitions when the ultrasonic sensor encounters partial pipe fill that prevents acoustic signal propagation between transducers
rows.Litmus Test: YES — Coriolis sensors degrade gradually rather than total signal loss on partial fill

rows.Statement: Minimize the measurement error caused by disturbed flow profiles when the ultrasonic sensor is installed with less than 5×DN straight pipe run in compact commercial machine enclosures with elbows and T-junctions upstream
rows.Litmus Test: YES — Coriolis meters are orientation-independent; electromagnetic tolerate 3×DN upstream

rows.Statement: Minimize the measurement dead zone at trickle-fill and pre-rinse flow rates below 4.2 l/min (DN20) where ultrasonic transit-time difference falls below the signal-to-noise detection threshold
rows.Litmus Test: YES — electromagnetic meters maintain signal at turndown 100:1 vs ultrasonic ~10:1

rows.Statement: Minimize the transient measurement error during rapid temperature cycling between cold fill (5°C) and hot wash (85°C) where ultrasonic speed-of-sound correction lags behind actual medium temperature change rate
rows.Litmus Test: YES — electromagnetic flow measurement is independent of fluid temperature for velocity measurement

rows.Statement: Minimize the risk of polymer housing seal degradation when commercial hot wash cycles operate at 85°C within 5°C of the ultrasonic sensor's 90°C material limit for extended duty cycles
rows.Litmus Test: YES — stainless-steel-housed electromagnetic and Coriolis sensors withstand 150°C+

rows.Statement: Minimize the likelihood of acoustic coupling degradation when surfactant concentrations in commercial wash water shift the medium's acoustic impedance outside the ultrasonic transducer's validated operating envelope
rows.Litmus Test: YES — electromagnetic meters measure velocity via Faraday's law, unaffected by chemical additives

rows.Why Not Applicable: Commercial equipment water circuits operate at 2–6 bar (municipal supply). High-pressure car wash spray circuits (50–150 bar) are excluded from sensor scope — different measurement point required [SRC: JTBD §1.5]

rows.Note: All 8 needs disappear with a different technology (electromagnetic or Coriolis)
Each constraint exists in 01_product_decomposition; causal mechanism is specific to ultrasonic transit-time operating principle
All constraints manifest in 333318: detergent water (air bubbles, surfactant), compact enclosures (straight run), rapid thermal cycling (5–85°C), drain transitions (pipe full)
7 of 8 constraints generate needs; 1 excluded with rationale (pressure threshold never reached in sensor-applicable circuits)
None duplicate Step 06 P2 market needs from JTBD
Range 6–8 imp / 3–5 sat; air-bubble needs score highest (imp=8) due to frequent manifestation in detergent/reclaim water

rows.Rationale: Marquardt offers 4 variants (DN12/14/20/25) sharing RAST 2.5 electronics; electromagnetic vendors offer DN3–DN300+. DN20/DN25 cover >70% of commercial equipment circuits, but incumbent range is objectively broader. DN12 (ice machines) still in development. Addresses N21 (variant count, Opp=8).

Marquardt offers UART/LIN/frequency — matching commercial equipment OEM microcontrollers directly. LIN enables cross-platform OEM development (automotive + commercial). Incumbent turbine sensors provide only passive frequency/pulse output requiring OEM counting/conversion logic. Electromagnetic meters offer 4-20 mA/HART (industrial protocols uncommon in commercial equipment controllers).

Marquardt's plug-in quick-connect reduces OEM assembly time from 2–3 min (wrench-and-seal threaded) to <30 s (snap-in). For OEMs producing 100+ machines/month, this translates to measurable production cost reduction. Incumbent turbine sensors offer threaded connections only. Addresses N17 (integration time, Opp=9).

<5 μA standby is well-implemented but commercial water-using machinery is mains-powered. Potential niche for remote standalone car wash water reclaim monitoring. No incumbent addresses this because the need rarely arises in this market. Superior implementation in an irrelevant context.

Marquardt factory-calibrates to customer's specific water/detergent composition and tests across operating flow profile with 100% automated final test. OEMs receive plug-and-produce sensors — no assembly-line calibration step. E+H ships with generic water calibration; field commissioning needed for non-standard media. Addresses N13 (commissioning time, Opp=7) and N17 (integration time, Opp=9).

KTW/WRAS enables deployment in potable water circuits within commercial water purification and ice machine segments (segments 5–6). For laundry/car wash (segments 1–4), irrelevant. Electromagnetic meters from major brands carry NSF 61 (US) alongside European certifications. For European OEMs, Marquardt's coverage is fully adequate. \

rows.Note: 4 superior, 1 parity, 1 inferior — 67% superior. Justified: comparison is against turbine incumbent (simple, cheap) for OEM integration features where Marquardt's sophistication provides genuine advantage
1 inferior on pipe size range is genuine (electromagnetic covers DN3–DN300+; Marquardt limited to DN12–DN25)
Best incumbent per feature: E+H Promag (market-leading electromagnetic) for size/calibration/cert; turbine/paddle-wheel (45–55% share incumbent) for communication/mechanical
All gap calculations verified. Superior requires >1pt vendor-over-incumbent; parity requires
Must_be (Comm Interface): both scores ≥ 5 ✓. Attractive (Pre-Calibrated): vendor > incumbent ✓. Indifferent scores reflect capability not market relevance ✓
Each rationale references concrete specs (DN sizes, protocol names, assembly times, calibration methods)

rows.Marquardt Advantage: Eliminates OEM assembly-line calibration — reduces per-unit integration time
UART/LIN/frequency matches commercial OEM controllers directly
rows.Marquardt Disadvantage: No 4-20 mA or Modbus for industrial-grade PLCs

rows.Marquardt Advantage: Plug-in quick-connect cuts assembly time from minutes to seconds

rows.Marquardt Disadvantage: DN12 still in development; no DN50+ for large commercial systems
Irrelevant for >95% of mains-powered commercial equipment

entities.naics_title: Other Commercial and Service Industry Machinery Manufacturing
entities.market_name: Other Commercial and Service Industry Machinery Manufacturing

entries.market_name: Other Commercial and Service Industry Machinery Manufacturing
entries.rationale: Directly delivers the commodity FP — measuring water flow is the reason OEMs buy a flow sensor. Without it, no billing, no water-use reporting, no fault detection. Primary drivers: Reliability (7) — replaces timed-fill guesswork (±10-20% vs ±1-3%); Confidence (7) — measured data for pay-per-use billing and WaterSense certification. Grounded in N01 (Opp=14) and N08 (Opp=13). Must_be because every flowmeter provides this.

entries.market_name: Other Commercial and Service Industry Machinery Manufacturing
entries.rationale: Commercial washers cycle 5-85 C. Integrated temperature enables thermal energy tracking and temperature-compensated flow accuracy without separate NTC probe. Primary drivers: Cost (5) — eliminates separate temp sensor BOM; Reliability (5) — enables compensation reducing error from ~1.5% to ~0.2%. Attractive because incumbents (turbine, timed fill) provide no temperature measurement. Grounded in N09 (Opp=9).

entries.market_name: Other Commercial and Service Industry Machinery Manufacturing
entries.rationale: Sensor flow restriction reduces available pump capacity for washing/rinsing. Less restriction = proportionally more capacity available (linear). Primary driver: Cost (5) — eliminates head-loss penalty in multi-machine installations. One_dimensional because benefit scales linearly with restriction reduction.

entries.market_name: Other Commercial and Service Industry Machinery Manufacturing
entries.rationale: The #1 pain point. Turbine sensors (45-55% share) clog from lint, detergent residue, and mineral scale within 12 months. Solid-state measurement with no maintenance is genuinely delightful. Primary drivers: Reliability (9) — eliminates dominant failure mode (N10 rotor clogging, Opp=14); Cost (8) — eliminates $150-300 quarterly field service calls (N11, Opp=11); Pain (8) — removes quarterly sensor disassembly. Attractive because market accepts mechanical wear as normal.

entries.market_name: Other Commercial and Service Industry Machinery Manufacturing
entries.rationale: Relevant for detecting check-valve failures (causing backflow and water waste), monitoring recycled water flow direction, and preventing potable water contamination in purification equipment. Primary drivers: Safety (6) — detects backflow risk in NSF-regulated purification; Cost (5) — identifies water waste from failed check valves. Attractive because OEMs don't expect directional sensing — check valves are used instead. Grounded in N06 (Opp=13).

entries.market_name: Other Commercial and Service Industry Machinery Manufacturing
entries.rationale: Detects bubbles, empty pipes, and medium composition changes. Empty-pipe detection within 5s prevents dry-run pump/heater damage in unmanned vended laundry and car wash. Primary drivers: Safety (8) — prevents pump destruction from dry-run (N04, Opp=14); Stress (7) — eliminates hidden-failure anxiety in unmanned equipment; Confidence (7) — definitive diagnostic flags replace manual inspection. Attractive because turbine and timed-fill incumbents have zero diagnostic capability. Grounded in N04 (Opp=14) and N15b (Opp=14).

entries.market_name: Other Commercial and Service Industry Machinery Manufacturing
entries.rationale: Compensates for temperature (5-85 C), detergent concentration, and recycled water property variation. Better compensation = proportionally better accuracy. Primary drivers: Reliability (7) — maintains accuracy across rapid thermal cycling (N09, Opp=9); Confidence (7) — users trust compensated readings across variable water quality (N22, Opp=13). One_dimensional because 0.2% compensated error is proportionally better than 1.5% uncompensated.
